title: The Greeks: Delta and Gamma Explored
author: Jose C S
keyHighlights: Delta measures the rate of change of an option's price relative to a $1 move in the underlying asset.
Gamma measures the acceleration of Delta, showing how Delta changes per dollar change in the underlying.
Understanding Gamma exposure is critical for hedging market maker positions during volatile expiry sessions.

content.text: For option traders, understanding the underlying asset price is only half the battle. To manage risk and project payouts accurately, one must master the Greeks—mathematical values that measure the sensitivity of options prices to changes in market parameters.

Delta ranges from 0 to 1 for Call options (and 0 to -1 for Puts). A Delta of 0.60 means that if the underlying stock rises by $1.00, the option premium will rise by approximately $0.60. Delta is also frequently used as a rough proxy for the probability of the option expiring in-the-money.

content.caption: Delta sensitivity curves shift dynamically as the underlying price approaches the strike.

content.text: Gamma represents the rate of change of Delta. If an option has a Delta of 0.50 and a Gamma of 0.10, a $1.00 increase in the stock will push the Delta up to 0.60. Gamma is highest for at-the-money options close to expiration, making options prices extremely sensitive to minor movements in the final trading hours.
By monitoring Gamma and Delta exposure, options strategy builders can adjust their positions dynamically, protecting their portfolios from sudden adverse market movements.
